text: I felt that the reading from this week did a good job of highlighting both the “good” and the “bad” of the climate crisis. Klein was able to capture the great destruction that we are currently experiencing, saying Earth is, “in the midst of its sixth mass extinction (the previous mass extinction, 65 million years ago, ended the dinosaurs).” (Klein, 2021) For most, this comparison should cause an immediate reaction. Extinction is not something that should be taken lightly, yet, we are amidst the highest CO2 levels and hottest temperatures ever recorded during the history of human life. However , Klein achieves what I feel a lot of authors fail to do: the positives. After demonstrating the crisis we are in, he highlights the fact that, “The flood of warnings from the world’ s scientists has not been ignored. The global environmental movement has grown by leaps and bounds…” (Klein 2021) A lot of readings I have done focus the entire time on the tragedy we are facing and almost making it feel helpless. I really appreciate how the author takes time to say that this fight is nowhere near over and more than ever people are stepping up to fight this issue. Furthermore, the author provides examples of the technology we currently have in order to achieve zero emissions, a feat once thought impossible. Klein quotes Mark Jacobson, Director of the Atmosphere/Energy Program at Stanford, who has, “roadmaps for 139 Countries and the 50 United States to T ransition to 100% Clean, Renewable Wind, W ater , and Solar Power for all Purposes by 2050 and 80% by 2030.” (Klein, 2021) Knowing that we have solutions in place to prevent a tragedy is a relieving feeling. Nevertheless, this is only the beginning, a plan can only do so much. Action is what really brings change. W atching the documentary , “Cowspiracy” made me realize how many dif ferent things are playing into the destruction of our environment. Seeing the varying ways humans are destroying our environment is sickening and makes me wonder what it will finally take for change to happen. The narrator quotes, “Free-living animals, 10,000 years ago, made up 99% of the biomass. And human beings, we only made up 1% of the biomass. Today , only 10,000 years later , which is really just a fraction of time, we human beings and the animals that we own as property make up 98% of the biomass. And wild, free-living animals make up only 2%.” To think that humans only make up a small fraction of our biomass but are causing immense destruction is a catastrophe. To make matters worse, the blame is being put on the wrong people. I personally feel that I hear a lot of people explaining that we need to reduce our water usage, food waste, etc. Of course this is true and we should be aware of our waste, the real blame needs to be upon the large industries such as agriculture and oil. The documentary states, “So much attention is given to lowering our home water use, yet domestic water use is only 5% of what is consumed in the US versus 55% for animal agriculture.” This dif ference is remarkable. This is a typical diversion tactic in which big industries put the blame on something small in order to take eyes of f of what is actually happening. I am happy to see that the truth is finally being revealed and now more than ever are we coming together to make a dif ference. As carbon dioxide levels and temperature continue to rise, we are getting closer to living on an uninhabitable planet. Climate change is already happening at an all time high. Issues such as sea levels rising concerningly fast and weather patterns like drought and hurricanes are just the beginning of this crisis. The documentary explains, “The world’ s climate scientists tell us that the highest safe level of emissions would be around 350 parts per million of carbon dioxide and greenhouse gasses in the atmosphere. W e’re already at 400.” Right away this documentary opens with the truth. W e are on the brink of a major disaster . W e have already crossed the assumed threshold for carbon dioxide and we are seeing the ef fects in real time. What happens when the carbon dioxide levels continue to rise? That may be a question I don’t want the answer to. Similar to the article, I appreciate how this documentary provides solutions to the problem. Easy ones at that. Anderson states, “A high-consuming , meat-eating Californian saves 1.4 tons of C02 equivalent per year by removing beef from their diet. They save 1.6 tons by going vegetarian. And 1.8 tons by going vegan.” This right here demonstrates how we finally do have the power to make change, even with something as simple as removing beef from our diet. Yes, the documentary does demonstrate the benefits of going completely vegetarian but for those who may find that too challenging, just removing beef from our diet can make a significant dif ference. Jacob Willison W ord Count: 831 WORKS CITED Andersen, Kip, and Keegan Kuhn. Cowspiracy : the SustainabilitySecret. [Santa Rosa, Calif.]:A.U.M.Films, 2014. Klein, Davis. “Ecosocialism and the Climate Crisis.” System Change Not Climate Change , 16 Sept. 2021, systemchangenotclimatechange.org/article/ecosocialism-and-climate-crisis/.
